era_context: The late 17th century: European powers consolidating colonial empires. The Ottoman Empire in decline; the Qing Dynasty at its height under Kangxi. Louis XIV dominates continental Europe. The English succession crisis is building. The scientific revolution is in full swing. The Monmouth Rebellion in England marks a turning point in English politics.

events.title: Monmouth's Rebellion and defeat
events.description: The Duke of Monmouth, an illegitimate son of Charles II, led a rebellion against King James II in an attempt to seize the throne. Monmouth landed in Dorset in June 1685 and raised a force of supporters, but was defeated at the Battle of Sedgemoor on 6 July 1685. Monmouth was captured and executed on 15 July 1685. The rebellion was a significant event in English politics, as it represented the last serious attempt to prevent a Catholic succession. The rebellion's failure strengthened James II's position temporarily, but the harsh reprisals (the 'Bloody Assizes') alienated the political elite and contributed to the Glorious Revolution in 1688. The rebellion is sometimes called the 'Monmouth Rebellion' or the 'Rye House Plot aftermath'.
key_figures: King James II (defender of the throne)
Judge George Jeffreys (judge in the trials)

sources.name: Contemporary accounts by participants and observers

events.certainty_note: The Monmouth Rebellion is precisely dated to June–July 1685. The Battle of Sedgemoor occurred on 6 July 1685 (Julian calendar). The rebellion is extensively documented in contemporary sources.

events.title: Bloody Assizes: Judge Jeffreys' trials of Monmouth rebels
events.description: Following the defeat of Monmouth's Rebellion, Judge George Jeffreys conducted a series of trials of the rebels and their supporters in the Southwest of England. The trials, known as the 'Bloody Assizes', were characterised by harsh sentences, including executions and transportations to the colonies. Jeffreys' conduct was widely seen as arbitrary and cruel, and the trials became a symbol of James II's tyranny. The Bloody Assizes alienated the political elite and contributed to the Glorious Revolution in 1688. The trials resulted in hundreds of convictions and dozens of executions, creating a climate of fear and resentment in the Southwest.
events.certainty_note: The Bloody Assizes are dated to 1685–1686 and are well-documented in court records and contemporary accounts.
cross_references: Led to 1689-003 (Reversal of convictions under William III)

events.title: Revocation of the Edict of Nantes by Louis XIV
events.description: King Louis XIV of France revoked the Edict of Nantes on 18 October 1685, ending the legal toleration of Protestantism in France. The revocation led to the persecution of French Huguenots (Protestants) and forced many to flee France. The revocation was a major religious and political event, as it represented the triumph of Catholic absolutism and the elimination of religious pluralism in France. The revocation had significant consequences for French society and economy, as many skilled Huguenots emigrated to Protestant countries (England, the Dutch Republic, Prussia, and others), taking their skills and capital with them. The revocation is sometimes seen as a symbol of Louis XIV's absolutism and religious intolerance.
events.certainty_note: The Revocation of the Edict of Nantes is precisely dated to 18 October 1685 (Julian calendar). The revocation is extensively documented in contemporary sources.
cross_references: Led to Huguenot emigration and diaspora

events.title: Great Turkish War: Ottoman-Habsburg conflict begins
events.region: Ottoman Empire and Holy Roman Empire (Hungary, Balkans)
events.description: The Great Turkish War (1683–1699) continued in 1685 with ongoing conflict between the Ottoman Empire and the Holy Roman Empire. The war had begun in 1683 with the Ottoman siege of Vienna, which was relieved by Polish and allied forces. In 1685, Habsburg forces pursued aggressive campaigns against Ottoman-held territories in Hungary and the Balkans. The war was part of the broader Ottoman decline in the late 17th century and would eventually result in the Treaty of Karlowitz (1699), which significantly reduced Ottoman territorial holdings in Europe.
events.certainty_note: The 1685 campaigns in the Great Turkish War are well-documented in both Habsburg and Ottoman sources.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor consolidates power over Oboi faction
events.description: The Kangxi Emperor consolidated his personal power by eliminating the influence of the Oboi faction, a group of Manchu nobles who had wielded significant power during the early Qing period. By 1685, Kangxi had fully established his personal authority and eliminated the last serious internal political threat to his rule. This consolidation of power allowed Kangxi to pursue his ambitious agenda of territorial expansion, cultural integration of Han Chinese elites, and administrative reform. The elimination of the Oboi faction was a crucial moment in establishing Kangxi's dominance and setting the stage for his long and successful reign.
key_figures: Oboi (deceased regent, but his faction still relevant)

sources.name: Kangxi Emperor's edicts and court records
Official Qing chronicles (Veritable Records of the Kangxi Emperor)

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Kangxi's power is well-documented in Qing archives. The elimination of the Oboi faction was essentially complete by 1685.
cross_references: Led to 1689-001 (Treaty of Nerchinsk, Kangxi's authority now secure)

events.title: Morean War: Venetian-Ottoman conflict continues
events.description: The Morean War (1684–1699) continued in 1685 with ongoing conflict between Venetian and Ottoman forces. Venetian forces under Francesco Morosini pursued military campaigns against Ottoman-held territories in the Peloponnese and the Aegean. The war was part of the broader decline of Ottoman power in the Mediterranean and southeastern Europe. The 1685 campaigns were part of the larger conflict that would eventually result in the Treaty of Karlowitz (1699).
events.certainty_note: The 1685 campaigns in the Morean War are well-documented in Venetian and Ottoman sources.

events.title: Aurangzeb's conquest of Golconda Sultanate
events.description: The Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb completed the conquest of the Golconda Sultanate in 1685, incorporating another major independent sultanate of the Deccan into the Mughal Empire. The conquest was the culmination of years of military campaigns and represented a major expansion of Mughal territorial control. The conquest of Golconda brought the Mughal Empire closer to its maximum territorial extent and consolidated Aurangzeb's control over the Indian subcontinent. However, the conquest came at a significant cost in terms of military resources and created internal tensions due to Aurangzeb's religious policies.
key_figures: Abul Hasan Qutb Shah (Golconda sultan, deposed)
events.certainty_note: The conquest of Golconda is dated to 1685 and is well-documented in Mughal records.

events.title: English succession crisis: James II becomes king
events.description: King James II ascended to the English throne in February 1685 following the death of his brother Charles II. James II was a Catholic, and his accession was controversial among the Protestant political elite. James II's reign was marked by attempts to promote Catholicism and to establish a Catholic dynasty, which alarmed the Protestant nobility and gentry. The Monmouth Rebellion in June 1685 was partly a reaction to James II's accession. James II's reign would be short-lived, ending with the Glorious Revolution in 1688. The year 1685 marked the beginning of a political crisis that would reshape English and British politics.
events.certainty_note: James II's accession is precisely dated to 6 February 1685 (Julian calendar). The accession is extensively documented in contemporary sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The Monmouth Rebellion is sometimes portrayed as a popular uprising against James II's Catholicism, but modern scholarship emphasises that the rebellion had limited popular support and was largely an elite factional conflict. The rebellion's failure was partly due to lack of popular support and partly due to superior royal military forces. The Bloody Assizes are sometimes portrayed as entirely arbitrary and unjust, but modern scholarship emphasises that the trials followed legal procedures (however harsh) and that the convictions were based on evidence of participation in the rebellion. The Revocation of the Edict of Nantes is sometimes portrayed as a sudden and unexpected event, but modern scholarship emphasises that it was the culmination of decades of increasing pressure on Huguenots and that it was widely anticipated by contemporaries. The Great Turkish War is sometimes portrayed as a straightforward Ottoman decline, but modern scholarship emphasises that the Ottoman Empire remained a formidable military power and that the war was a complex conflict with multiple causes and outcomes.
historiographic_note: The year 1685 is exceptionally well-documented in European sources, particularly for England, France, and the Ottoman Empire. Chinese sources are also rich for the Kangxi period. However, documentation is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events. The Mughal Empire is documented in both Mughal and European sources, but the European perspective is limited to coastal trading posts. Non-elite perspectives, women's experiences, and the impact on common people are underrepresented across all regions. The Huguenot emigration following the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes is documented in both French and receiving countries' sources, but the experiences of individual Huguenots are less well-documented.
